title: RNA-Protein Interaction Detection (RaPID)
description: {"blocks":[{"key":"ps8za","text":"RNA-Protein interactions play numerous roles in cellular function and disease. Here we describe RNA-protein interaction detection (RaPID), which uses proximity-dependent protein labeling, based on the BirA biotin ligase, to rapidly identify the proteins that bind RNA sequences of interest in living cells. RaPID displays utility in multiple applications, including in evaluating protein binding to mutant RNA motifs in human genetic disorders, in uncovering potential post-transcriptional networks in breast cancer, and in discovering essential host proteins that interact with Zika virus RNA. To improve the BirA labeling component of RaPID, moreover, a new mutant BirA was engineered from B. subtilis that enables \u003e1000-fold faster kinetics and \u003e30-fold increased signal-to-noise ratio over the prior standard E. coli BirA, thereby enabling direct study of RNA-protein interactions in living cells on a timescale as short as one minute.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"bold","offset":205,"length":409},{"style":"bold","offset":671,"length":154}],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}}],"entityMap":{}}
guidelines: {"blocks":[{"key":"j1tad","text":"RNA-protein interactions are pervasive in biological processes and play a critical role in regulating cellular function1,2. Approximately 5% of the human proteome has been experimentally identified as capable of binding RNA and recent work has revealed several networks of RNA-protein interactions that regulate genomic stability and cellular signaling in addition to gene expression3–5. Consequently, there is growing interest in utilizing synthetic RNAs as drugs and as tools to control cellular circuits6,7.\r","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"sup","offset":119,"length":3},{"style":"sup","offset":383,"length":5},{"style":"sup","offset":506,"length":3}],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"xixgu","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"7cjwn","text":"Discovery and study of RNA-protein interactions has lagged the growing appreciation of their biological importance due to limitations in existing methods. Current methods rely on purifying RNA of interest to discover protein binding partners lead to biases  owing  to  post-lysis  re-assortments  and  generate  high  false  positives (Supplementary  Table   1)8,9.  RNAs  fold  and  adopt  complex  structures  in  cellular environment  and  such  structures  are  critical  in  mediating  interactions  with  proteins10.\r","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"sup","offset":361,"length":3},{"style":"sup","offset":519,"length":2}],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"gyrit","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"ytwu8","text":"\r","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"oorur","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"qg0bp","text":"Methods to study known RNA-protein interactions just as RNA-MaP \u0026 RNA-compete are performed in artificial settings that do not provide conditions reflective of RNA-protein interaction in living cells and require specialized equipment (Supplementary Table 1)11.\r","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"sup","offset":257,"length":2}],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"udhjp","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"6tbev","text":"To overcome these technical limitations, a methodology was developed to directly tag and isolate proteins that bind to specific RNA motifs in intact live cells. A modified promiscuous biotin ligase from E. coli, BirA, biotinylates proximal proteins within a range of 10nm and has been used to study protein-protein interactions12,13. Biotinylation of target proteins permits stringent washing and reduces background. Phage biology was leveraged to design a tool utilizing BirA to biotin tag proteins bound to RNA motifs in living cells followed by streptavidin pull-down and mass spectrometry to identify the proteins, terming it RaPID for RNA-Protein Interaction Detection.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"bold","offset":216,"length":271},{"style":"sup","offset":327,"length":5}],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}}],"entityMap":{}}

steps: Clone BoxB stem loop (GCCCTGAAAAAGGGC) adjacent to RNA of interest
Depending on the cell type, transfect or infect BoxB. In this protocol, we will use HEK293T and RaPID labeler plasmid (E.coli BirA*) Day 0 - Plate 300K HEK293T cells per well in a 6 well plate
Day 1 - Transfect 2ug of pMotif plasmid with 0.3ug of RaPID labeler plasmid
Day 2 - Make labeling media. Dilute 20x Biotin (4mM Biotin) with DMEM to make labeling media. Aspirate out existing media and add labeling media to each well.
Day 3 - Processing Samples
Make Lysis buffer fresh. Wash Buffer 1 can be made and stored at room temperature. Wash Buffers 2-4 can be made in bulk and stored at 4C.
Aspirate out labeling media. Add cold 1xPBS and gently mix. Aspirate out 1xPBS
Add 500uL cold 1xPBS to each well and scrape cells. Collect in individual eppendorf tubes
Spin cells down at 500g at 4C
Asplirate out PBS STOP Point : Eppendorf tubes can be stored at -80C for processing later
Lyse cells in each tube with 200uL Lysis buffer at room temperature.
Add 20uL 25% Triton X-100. Mix thoroughly
Add 220uL cold Wash Buffer 4
Sonicate samples throughly - 10% for 10s. Ensure lysate is clear after sonication. If lysate is not clear, cool for 5s and repeat sonication
Spin lysate at 15,000 g at 4C for 10 minutes
Carefully transfer supernatant to 3K MWCO filter tube
Spin filter tubes at 1500g at 4C for 1hour
Pipette retente into new eppendorf tubes.
Estimate protein concentration in each tube and use Wash Buffer 4 to equalize concentration across all samples
After equalizing concentration, pipette 20uL into a new tube and label as Lysate_Sample

Calculate amount of protein in each sample. Calculate the amount of beads required for each sample. Use 50uL of MyOne C1 Streptavidin beads per mg of protein. Ensure a minimum of 30uL of beads is used per sample to capture biotinylated proteins and account for loss of beads during processing.
Wash the MyOne C1 beads in Wash Buffer 4 x 2
Prepare fresh set of tubes and label them Pulldown_Sample. Add equal volume of sample and beads to each tube.
End-over-end rotation for 1hr at room temperature OR overnight at 4C Washing
Place tubes on magnetic stand and aspirate out lysate
Add 1mL of Wash Buffer 1 to each tube and perform end-over-end rotation for 5 mins
Place tubes on magnetic stand and aspirate out Wash Buffer 1
Add 1mL of Wash Buffer 1 to each tube and perform end-over-end rotation for 5 mins
Place tubes on magnetic stand and aspirate out Wash Buffer 1
Add 1mL of Wash Buffer 2 to each tube and perform end-over-end rotation for 5 mins
Place tubes on magnetic stand and aspirate out Wash Buffer 2
Add 1mL of Wash Buffer 3 to each tube and perform end-over-end rotation for 5 mins
Place tubes on magnetic stand and aspirate out Wash Buffer 3
Add 1mL of Wash Buffer 4 to each tube and perform end-over-end rotation for 5 mins
Place tubes on magnetic stand and aspirate out Wash Buffer 4
Add 40uL of Elution Buffer to each tube and heat at 98C for 20 minutes.
Run Western Blot for Pulldown & Lysate for samples
EDEN15 is an RNA motif that binds to CELF1 protein. Using EDEN15 and scrambled controls, RaPID-Western was performed. Figure 2 shows RaPID-Western results with pulldown and lysates.
None
Figure 1. RaPID Schema Figure 2. RaPID-Western EDEN15 RaPID-Western of EDEN15 vs two scrambled sequence controls of identical nucleotide composition (scr1 and scr2)
